method_atom: A self-consistent local-field correction in which each discrete unit embedded in a continuous polarizable medium responds not to the average (macroscopic) field but to an effective field renormalized by the surrounding medium, producing a multiplicative shift in the unit's characteristic resonance. Two alternative theoretical frameworks for the same effect — a coarse-grained macroscopic continuum theory versus a microscopically resolved discrete-unit theory — yield different correction factors, and the contradiction is adjudicated by demanding consistency with a limiting/correspondence principle, which selects the microscopic (discrete) result over the macroscopic (continuum) one.
object_atom: A dense collection of embedded two-level atoms in a dielectric host medium interacting with the quantized electromagnetic field, specifically the Lorentz redshift of the atomic resonance frequency and whether it is enhanced by the refractive index n (macroscopic quantum electrodynamics) or by (n^2+2)/3 (the classical Lorentz local-field condition applied to the optical Bloch equations, confirmed by microscopic quantum electrodynamics).
brief_reason: The transferable part is the self-consistent local-field renormalization of embedded units and the macroscopic-vs-microscopic framework discrepancy resolved by a correspondence-principle limit; the domain-specific part is the two-level atoms in a dielectric, the electromagnetic field, and the specific refractive-index enhancement factors.
